prompt: Share an untranslatable word or idiom from any language you know about — something that doesn't have a clean English equivalent — and explain what it captures.
response:
Portuguese has a tiny, everyday adjective that English simply has no room for: **saudoso**.

Formed from the noun *saudade*, it is applied to anything (or anyone) that is **“being missed right now”**.  
A *“filme saudoso”* is not a “nostalgic film” in the sense that the film feels nostalgia; it is a film that the speaker actively misses, a film whose absence hurts a little.  
A *“avó saudosa”* is a grandmother who is gone and whose presence is painfully felt in the room at this very moment.  

English can say “I miss my grandmother” or “My grandmother is dearly missed,” but it has no single word that turns the verb “to miss” back into an adjective and pins the emotion on the person or thing that is gone. *Saudoso* packs the missingness into the absent object itself: the grandmother carries her own missed-ness.
